resources.string:
During a check, depending on enabled checks and permissions, the app may make network requests to these endpoints:
• api.beacondb.net
• api.ipapi.is
• www.iplocate.io
• api.ipquery.io
• www.iplookup.it
• api.ipbot.com
• ip.mail.ru
• api4.ipify.org
• api6.ipify.org
• 2ip.ru
• ipv4-internet.yandex.net
• ipv6-internet.yandex.net
• api.sypexgeo.net
• ifconfig.me
• checkip.amazonaws.com
• api.ipify.org
• api-ipv4.ip.sb
• api-ipv6.ip.sb

If split tunnel bypass, local proxy scan, or Xray API scan is enabled, the app may connect to local loopback endpoints:
• 127.0.0.1 on selected ports
• ::1 on selected ports
Popular scans include ports 1080, 1081, 1443, 2080, 2081, 3067, 7890, 7891, 8080, 10085, 10808, 10809, and 12334; full scans use the configured range.

If a preset DNS resolver is selected, the app may also connect to these DNS endpoints:
• 1.1.1.1
• 1.0.0.1
• 8.8.8.8
• 8.8.4.4
• 77.88.8.8
• 77.88.8.1
• cloudflare-dns.com
• dns.google
• common.dot.dns.yandex.net

If a custom DNS resolver is selected, the app may also connect to its user-entered IP addresses or DoH domain.

If CDN pulling is enabled, the app may additionally send HTTPS requests to these endpoints:
• redirector.googlevideo.com
• www.cloudflare.com
• one.one.one.one
• rutracker.org
• meduza.io

If Call transport leak probe is enabled, the app may additionally send UDP/STUN requests to these endpoints:
• stun.fitauto.ru:3478
• stun.sberbank.ru:3478
• stun.l.google.com:19302
• stun1.l.google.com:19302
• stun2.l.google.com:19302
• stun3.l.google.com:19302
• stun4.l.google.com:19302
• stun.cloudflare.com:3478
• global.stun.twilio.com:3478
• stun.nextcloud.com:443
• stun.sipgate.net:3478
• stun.voip.blackberry.com:3478
• stun.voipgate.com:3478
• stun.antisip.com:3478
• stun.sip.us:3478
• stun.1und1.de:3478
• stun.voipbuster.com:3478
• stun.counterpath.net:3478
• stun.ekiga.net:3478
• stun.stunprotocol.org:3478
• stun.schlund.de:3478
• stun.linphone.org:3478

For proxy-assisted Telegram MTProto probing, the app may ask a local SOCKS5 proxy to connect to:
• 149.154.167.51:443
• 149.154.167.41:443

If ICMP spoofing detection is enabled, the app may additionally resolve and ping:
• instagram.com
• google.com

If update checks are enabled, the app may contact api.github.com.

If Debug diagnostics is enabled, the app can also prepare a copyable diagnostics report from the last completed scan, even when the scan finished successfully. That report is kept only in memory until you copy it. CDN pulling responses are included in that report.

By tapping "Run check", you agree that these network requests will be made from your device.

The app does not keep logs of these requests. Network access can be disabled in the app settings.
Port scanning takes a long time
Keeps an in-memory diagnostics snapshot from the last completed scan, including CDN pulling responses, and enables the Copy diagnostics button on the main screen.
Overrides the TUN public-IP probe method used during split tunnel checks.
Scans localhost for open SOCKS5/HTTP proxy ports.
Scans localhost for exposed Xray gRPC API ports.
Test on-device proxies for weak credentials and UDP relay (loopback only)
Used by enabled localhost proxy and Xray scans.
GeoIP, IP comparison, CDN pulling, BeaconDB, call transport probe, ICMP spoofing
Checks api.github.com for GitHub Releases and offers to download a newer APK.

If disabled:

• GeoIP checks will not run
• IP address comparison via external services will be disabled
• CDN pulling checks will be disabled
• Geolocation via BeaconDB (cell towers/Wi-Fi) will be disabled
• Call transport leak probing will be disabled
• ICMP spoofing checks will be disabled

Only local checks will remain available: direct signs, indirect signs, and port scanning
Sends HTTPS requests to Google/YouTube redirector and Cloudflare trace endpoints.

The app will send normal web requests to Google/YouTube, www.cloudflare.com, one.one.one.one, rutracker.org and meduza.io. These sites may see your device public IP and return short network details like country, datacenter, or proxy mode.
Send a request to meduza.io/cdn-cgi/trace as part of CDN pulling.
Sends UDP/STUN requests to public STUN servers (global and Russian) and probes local SOCKS5 proxy for Telegram MTProto traffic.
Used for GeoIP, IP Comparison, and BeaconDB. It does not change the Android system DNS and does not affect local DNS checks.
App icon updated. On some launchers it may briefly disappear and re-appear.
Telegram DC is unreachable through the local SOCKS5 proxy
Targets for this check are unavailable
Permission is blocked. Open the app settings.

For a more accurate check, the app needs additional permissions.

They are used to read cell tower identifiers and perform geolocation lookups via BeaconDB.

%s

Without these permissions, the check will still run, but some location signals, Wi-Fi scanning, and dual-SIM diagnostics will be unavailable.
On Android 13+, the app also requests access to nearby Wi-Fi devices to discover nearby Wi-Fi access points.
The app also uses Wi-Fi scanning to discover nearby access points.
The app also requests phone state access to read active SIM subscriptions for dual-SIM diagnostics.
Check in progress. Cards update live as data arrives.
Country, ASN, and hosting indicators will be checked.
The public IP will be compared through multiple independent services.
It will be checked what blocked or CDN-backed endpoints return about your network.
A search for direct signs of VPNs, proxies, and installed clients will be performed.
Interfaces, DNS, routes, and local technical signals will be checked.
Native interfaces, routes, and low-level library signals will be checked.
STUN server responses and call transport leak signals will be checked.
Blocked and control hosts will be resolved and pinged through the active route.
Local and foreign services will be pinged to compare round-trip latency.
Carrier, cell tower, and nearby Wi-Fi signals will be collected.
A search for local split tunnel bypasses and available proxy endpoints will be performed.
Configured domains will be tested for DPI-level blocking via DNS, TCP, and TLS handshake.
Checking country, ASN, and hosting indicators.
Comparing the public IP through multiple independent services.
Checking what blocked or CDN-backed endpoints return about your network.
Resolving and pinging blocked and control hosts through the active route.
Pinging local and foreign services to compare round-trip latency.
Looking for direct signs of VPNs, proxies, and installed clients.
Checking interfaces, DNS, routes, and local technical signals.
Checking native interfaces, routes, and low-level library signals.
Checking STUN server responses and call transport leak signals.
Collecting carrier, cell tower, and nearby Wi-Fi signals.
Looking for local split tunnel bypasses and available proxy endpoints.
Testing domain accessibility through DNS, TCP, and TLS handshake.
Scanning was stopped before this stage completed.
This stage was stopped before completion.
Localhost ports to scan for proxy and Xray API: %1$s (%2$s)
Localhost ports to scan for proxy: %1$s (%2$s)
Localhost ports to scan for Xray API: %1$s (%2$s)
Local proxy and Xray scans are disabled.
The automated check considers the bypass confirmed.
The automated check is not enough for a clear conclusion.
The check did not find convincing signs of a bypass.
The local Xray API exposed a remote endpoint address.
Only the public egress IP was visible, not the final server address.
Only a local proxy/API on the device was visible, without the remote endpoint address.
Only technical network and routing signals were available, without a server address.
Neither a server address nor a public IP could be obtained.
A red verdict means the automated check collected enough signs of a bypass.
A yellow verdict means signals exist, but they need manual verification.
A green verdict means no decisive bypass signals were found.
An actual remote endpoint address was obtained, not just a public IP.
A public IP and a final server address are not the same: internet services only see the egress IP, which may be just an exit point.
A local proxy/API indicates an on-device bypass mechanism, but does not reveal the remote server by itself.
Technical signals confirm network behavior, but do not reveal the remote endpoint address.
With the current data, it is not possible to tell whether the app learned the bypass server address.
A local Xray API was found on the device.
The direct IP and the IP via the local proxy differ.
With VPN active, the app was able to access the non-VPN network.
The app was able to use the VPN Network object separately from the regular network.
A local proxy was found, but the bypass was not automatically confirmed.
RU and non-RU IP checkers returned different public IPs.
External IP checkers responded incompletely or inconsistently.
Location signals point to Russia, while GeoIP points to an external address.
GeoIP indicates an external or suspicious address.
Direct signs of a local proxy/VPN were found.
Indirect signs of tunneling, routing changes, or DNS replacement were found.
A call transport endpoint responded and exposed mapped/public network data.
ICMP reachability or RTT looked inconsistent with a normal Russian route.
The verdict is based on a combination of multiple check signals.
Signals are partial or contradictory, so manual review is needed.
No decisive bypass signals were found.
A foreign SIM (%1$s, %2$s) is connected via a Russian visited network. The public IP belongs to the home operator\'s ASN, which is normal home-routed roaming and not a bypass.
A foreign SIM (%1$s) is connected via a Russian visited network. The public IP belongs to the home operator\'s ASN, which is normal home-routed roaming and not a bypass.
A foreign SIM (%1$s, %2$s) is connected via a Russian visited network. The data session likely exits via the home country, which can look like a bypass.
A foreign SIM (%1$s) is connected via a Russian visited network. The data session likely exits via the home country, which can look like a bypass.
A foreign SIM is connected via a Russian visited network. The data session may legitimately exit abroad (home-routed roaming).
You are scanning under operator whitelist mode — result may be distorted
Scan performed under operator whitelist mode — results may be incomplete
IP belongs to a hosting provider: %1$s
IP is listed in known proxy/VPN databases: %1$s
RU and non-RU checkers returned different IPs: %1$s and %2$s
RU and non-RU checkers returned addresses from different families: %1$s and %2$s
IPs differ, but the data is incomplete: %1$s and %2$s
All responding IPv4 checkers returned the same IP: %1$s
All checkers returned the same IP: %1$s
No IP checker returned a response
Comparison is incomplete: some checkers did not respond
IPv6 errors were ignored, but there is no valid IPv4 response
Services returned addresses from different families: %1$s
All responding services in the group returned IP %1$s
None of the CDN targets returned usable data
Different CDN targets exposed different IPs: %1$s
All CDN targets exposed the same IP: %1$s
%2$d of %3$d CDN targets responded and exposed IP %1$s
%1$d of %2$d CDN targets returned trace data, but not all responses included an IP
Could not verify the TLS certificate chain. Possible causes: HTTPS interception, a wrong DNS answer, or an outdated Android CA store.
%1$s: host found without a valid port
ProxyInfo on tracked networks: not detected
App is excluded from active per-app VPN (tun0 present but not accessible to this app)
VPN interfaces (tun/tap/wg/ppp or confirmed IPsec): none detected
Capabilities %1$s: NOT_VPN=%2$s, TRANSPORT_VPN=%3$s, IMS=%4$s, EIMS=%5$s, MMTEL=%6$s
Default route via a non-standard interface: %1$s
Default route (/proc/net/route) via a non-standard interface: %1$s
Routing indicates split tunneling: both direct and tunnel routes are visible
Gateway route: could not assess via Android API
DNS points to localhost: %1$s (typical for VPN/local proxy)
DNS: %1$s (inherited from the same private subnet as the main network)
DNS in a private subnet: %1$s (differs from the underlying network)
DNS was replaced while VPN is active: %1$s
DNS: %1$s (source could not be matched to the underlying network)
Multiple localhost listeners detected on high ports: %1$d
Additional technical proxy signs: none detected
Checks for processes, iptables/pf, and system certificates are limited without root/privileged access
Looking for open proxies on localhost…
Looking for a gRPC API on localhost…
Checking access to the non-VPN network…
Obtaining the direct IP and the IP via proxy…
Local SOCKS5 proxy %1$s accepts weak credentials
Local SOCKS5 proxy %1$s allows UDP ASSOCIATE without auth
Per-app split is disabled: IPs match
Proxy requires authentication: bypass status unconfirmed
MTProto proxy: Telegram DC is reachable via %1$s -> %2$s
MTProto probe: Telegram DC is unreachable via proxy
Open proxies on localhost: none detected
HandlerService/ListOutbounds is unavailable; outbound addresses were not exposed.
Underlying network: VPN is not active, the check is not required
VPN network binding: the app can reach both the default non-VPN path and VPN Network (VPN IP: %1$s, default IP: %2$s)
TUN active probe, %1$s, via proxy: IP %2$s
TUN active probe: a request via VPN Network is unavailable (the interface is blocked or filtered)
TUN active probe: a request via VPN Network failed (%1$s)
TUN active probe: strict same-path failed (%1$s), but the curl-compatible transport-only path succeeded; DNS path mismatch is likely
TUN active probe: strict same-path failed (%1$s), but the curl-compatible path succeeded; DNS path mismatch is likely
Underlying network: the non-VPN network is unavailable (full tunnel)
Underlying network: the non-VPN network is unavailable (%1$s)
VPN gateway leak: the app can bypass the VPN tunnel (VPN IP: %1$s, real IP: %2$s)
The underlying network is reachable, but the IP matches the VPN%1$s: split tunnel not confirmed
Split tunnel comparison returned different IP families (VPN IP: %1$s, non-VPN IP: %2$s): manual review is needed
Split tunnel comparison is incomplete (VPN IP: %1$s, non-VPN IP: %2$s): manual review is needed
Split tunnel comparison used a curl-compatible transport-only fallback for: %1$s
Split tunnel comparison used a curl-compatible fallback for: %1$s
%1$s debug: effective mode %2$s, selected mode %3$s, strict %4$s, curl-compatible %5$s, dnsPathMismatch %6$s
Known VPN/Proxy apps and VpnService providers: none detected
Run the check to see the verdict
Check runs locally \u00b7 network endpoints listed
%1$s did not reply, and %2$s stayed above the RTT threshold (avg %3$s)
%1$s replied to ICMP even though it is expected to stay silent on a Russian route
%1$s replied with suspiciously low RTT (min %2$s, avg %3$s)
%1$s replied and %2$s was too fast (min %3$s, avg %4$s)
ICMP check is inconclusive because %1$s did not reply
ICMP spoofing check is unavailable on this device
Control target %1$s (%2$s): %3$d/%4$d replies, min %5$s, avg %6$s, max %7$s
%1$s replied and %2$s RTT stayed below the expected threshold
%1$s replied over ICMP on the active route
%1$s RTT stayed below the expected threshold on the active route
Detects VPN by comparing latency to local vs foreign services
Tests domains for DPI-level blocking (DNS, TCP, TLS)
Local services slower than foreign — possible tunnel
RTT triangulation supports RU only in this build
Median latency: local %1$s ms, foreign %2$s ms (threshold %3$.0f ms)
A local median above the threshold suggests traffic is leaving the country before reaching local services.
High jitter on the majority of targets — confidence reduced.
Experimental: pings local and foreign servers to detect VPN by latency anomalies. May produce false positives on cellular, satellite, CGNAT, or congested networks.
Native library not loaded; native checks are unavailable.
Interface %1$s: visible in native but missing in JVM
Interface %1$s: visible in JVM but missing in native
Library symbols: all resolve to libc
Root indicators: no signs of root access detected
SELinux enforcement status is not accessible
Process is running with root UID: %1$s
Host route to %1$s via physical interface %2$s (VPN server IP leak)

On first launch the app can contact api.github.com to check GitHub Releases for a newer APK. If you disable this now, the app will not perform this check until you enable it manually in network settings.
Enable at least one check to save the profile
"%1$s" requires "%2$s" — it has been enabled automatically
Configured domains will be tested for DPI-level blocking via DNS, TCP, and TLS handshake.
Use the Domain reachability section for this type.
Marketplace coming soon.\n\nCommunity check profiles will be available here.
Open Settings → Custom Checks → Import to load the profile
This profile overrides your DNS settings. All DNS lookups during the scan will go through the server above instead of your configured DNS.
Profile rejected: hash does not match catalog

Profile "%1$s" is not verified by the repository maintainers. It contains the following custom network endpoints:

%2$s

Install only if you trust the author.
Enable this check to edit its parameters
JNI checks for routes, interfaces, hook markers and root indicators. No additional parameters.
When installed, this profile will send requests to the following URLs:
